title: Decimals
learning_objectives: Multiply and divide decimals by tracking decimal places and shifting by powers of 10.
Convert fluently among fractions, decimals, and percents.
Round and estimate to sanity-check the size of an answer.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do → we do → you do

opening.builds_on: Place value and the fraction ↔ decimal link.

items.prompt: Write 0.75 as a fraction in lowest terms.

items.answer: Multiply the digits (2 × 3 = 6), then place the point by the TOTAL decimal places (1 + 1 = 2).

retrieval_starter.purpose: Retrieves decimal–fraction conversions and the decimal-place rule for multiplication — the foundation for the division and size-estimation problems coming up.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: If place counting is shaky, model 0.2 × 0.3 as (2/10)(3/10) = 6/100 = 0.06 to anchor the rule in fractions.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: Q5 explains the rule you'll lean on all lesson: a decimal is just a fraction over a power of ten. Once you see 0.2 = 2/10, multiplying and dividing decimals becomes counting places and shifting by powers of 10 — and converting to fractions is often the fastest GMAT move.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can multiply and divide decimals reliably, convert among fractions/decimals/percents, and estimate to check an answer's size.
success_criteria: I can divide by a decimal by shifting both numbers to make the divisor whole.
I can convert a decimal to a reduced fraction and back.
I can predict roughly how big an answer should be before computing.

opening_script.move: Check; dwell on Q5's place-value reasoning.
Bridge: decimals are fractions over powers of 10; preview division by shifting.

i_do.stem: Which of the following is equal to 0.0036 ÷ 0.04?
i_do.explanation: Multiply both numbers by 100 to make the divisor whole: 0.0036 ÷ 0.04 = 0.36 ÷ 4 = 0.09.
think_aloud_steps: Dividing by a decimal is awkward; make the divisor a whole number.
Size check: dividing by the small number 0.04 should give a small result — 0.09 fits.
i_do.key_takeaway: To divide by a decimal, shift the decimal point in BOTH numbers until the divisor is a whole number.

we_do.explanation: Ignore the points: 12 × 5 = 60. Total decimal places = 1 + 2 = 3, so 0.060 = 0.06.
scaffold_hints: Count decimal places in the factors: 1 (in 1.2) + 2 (in 0.05) = 3.
Place the point 3 from the right: 0.060 = 0.06.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Right — 60 with three decimal places is 0.06.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Count places in BOTH factors (1 + 2 = 3), not just one.
Use the benchmark 1/8 = 0.125 and multiply by 7, or long-divide 7 ÷ 8.

sample_held_out_items.stem: Write 0.16 as a fraction in lowest terms.

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
